title: The Secret Behind YouTube's Biggest Channels

transcript.text: Stop chasing the YouTube algorithm, seriously. The creators who have built the biggest audiences on YouTube didn't do it by chasing trends and optimizing click-through rates. They did it through a framework, seven powerful elements I'm going to share with you in this video, so you can start dominating YouTube, too. Nah. IShowSpeed, whether you love him or you hate him, went from no audience whatsoever to 50 million subscribers. Markiplier, and let's be honest, most people love him, built his channel from his hospital bed after losing his job and his apartment all in the same year. And then we have Moist Critical, who built a 1 million subscriber channel with a anonymous disembodied voice no one knew the name of. >> Oh, that was a horrible kick. Horrible kick. Where did you learn to kick? The Institute of Shitty Kicking? >> So there we have it, three completely different channels with three completely different stories. But, when you look closely at each of these creators and how they built their channels into empires, a pattern emerges. And this pattern, to some extent, shows up on every successful YouTube channel there is. And it's called primal branding. Now, this concept isn't exclusive to YouTube and the creators on the platform. Some of the biggest entities in the world, Nike, Apple, Red Bull, Coldplay, Christianity, all use primal branding in one form or another. And your favorite YouTubers do, too, even if you don't realize it and even if they don't realize it. So what is primal branding? Well, you might be wondering why I didn't do something earlier on in this video. And this is why. It is a framework developed by Patrick Hanlon that treats brands as belief systems rather than merely products. >> These black and white geometric shapes mean nothing to us other than the fact that they're circle, square, and triangle, whereas these black and white geometric shapes mean a whole lot to us. >> It is designed to build communities and cultivate passionate followers. >> So, in the music world, think Swifties, and in the YouTube world, think PewDiePie and his bro army back in the day. >> That level of loyalty is not an accident. >> And by the end of this video, you'll be able to spot this primal branding from a mile away and infuse it into your own content so that you start to grow your channel and build a community around it. >> But, I do have to warn you, if you want a quick solution to your YouTube problems, then this probably isn't the video for you. >> Primal branding isn't a hack. It's not going to fix your videos entirely tomorrow, and it's not going to transform your channel by this time next week. >> But, if you do start to eat, sleep, create, and repeat these principles today, by this time next year, your YouTube journey, your channel, your community will be absolutely transformed. I can guarantee it. >> Let's talk about the logical first >> part of primal branding, which is the creation story. Every massive brand entity has that creation story. Apple was created in a garage. Google was created in a, you guessed it, garage. Amazon was created in a, you guessed it, garage or garage. >> Now, you might be wondering at this point, is Silicon Valley just obsessed with garages? >> But, >> none of this is a coincidence because all of these companies lead very intentionally with these stories on purpose. >> And that's because the creation story isn't just about where you came from. It's a reason for people to root for you. >> So, let's look at the biggest YouTuber on the platform right now, Mr. Beast. Close. Really, really close to half a billion subscribers. >> Well, many people will have a split opinion on Mr. Beast. The one thing that everybody can get behind is his creation story, which is essentially the same as yours, to find success on YouTube. So, Mr. Beast isn't just the chocolate entrepreneur that you know him today. Before all of that, he was simply Jimmy Donaldson. Since February 12th, 2012, and he admits this himself, he's spent pretty much every single waking moment focused on YouTube. And for several years and many hundreds of videos, he didn't get any views, but he never quit. He dropped out of college. He was even kicked out of a family home. But, against all the opposition the world threw at him, he persevered. He bet on himself, and he won big. That is the power of the origin story. It's your law. As for my creation story, the VidIQ one, it all goes back to this tweet I sent over a decade ago. I'd been making videos on my own channel for a few years at that point, and I wanted to share with people what tools had helped me grow. So, I made one about VidIQ's ability to copy and paste tags. A tweet about that video was seen by the co-founder of VidIQ, and we started working together. And it didn't take long for that VidIQ work to overtake what I was doing on my own channel. And soon, I had to make a tough decision. The opportunity was there to go in full-time with VidIQ, to turn my passion of content creation into a career. But, it meant sacrificing my own channel and making a financial leap of faith with no safety net. But, I bet on myself, and I think after a decade, I've come out ahead. And since then, my focus has been to help you do exactly the same thing on your YouTube channel with your mission. So, just stop for a moment and ask yourself this question. Does your audience know why you started? Not what you make on your channel, but why you started your channel. Because that is the critical difference between a YouTube channel people simply watch and a YouTube channel people actually root for. And if your answer to such a question actually feels quite vulnerable to share, that's probably a good thing. The most powerful creation stories come from a sense of vulnerability. So, don't think of this as a weakness, think of it as an opportunity to emotionally connect with your audience who probably share those same vulnerabilities with you. But, that is just the first piece of this complex puzzle. People don't just want to root for a story, they're hardwired to need a reason to believe in something. And that's where the creed comes into play. This is the core belief system at the heart of every brand and major YouTube channel. And the creed isn't really about what you make, it's more about what you stand for. And unlike the creation story, this isn't something that's said out loud, but it's demonstrated through the actions of the brand of YouTube channel. Take a look at Moist Critical, for example. He doesn't explicitly say it, but you can feel his creed. And a big huge note, not massive part of that creed is simply being 100% his authentic self. Take a look at the video packaging, for example. There's no real thought or intrinsic design that goes into them. It's instincts, and that instinct in itself has become iconic of his creed. He doesn't necessarily live in a place where all of the creator action takes place, such as LA, and he has surrendered absolutely none of his persona in the pursue of the algorithm or a certain lifestyle. And because of this raw and authentic and really powerful creed, his audience completely trusts him and turns up for him time and time again. And so, once you know what a creed looks like or more what a creed feels like, you'll start to see it showing up everywhere. As an example of something that lives both within YouTube and outside of YouTube, let's look at Red Bull. Their creed isn't to sell energy drinks. Their creed is the idea of the human potential having no ceiling. YouTube's core belief is, or at least I still think it is, the idea that anybody can build an audience around anything on the platform. And here at vidIQ, our creed is that every creator deserves a chance to be discovered. So, what do you stand for? Not again what you actually make, but what you actually believe in. So again, let's try and write or type this down in a single sentence. What is your creed? If you can't do that today, that's okay. That's not a problem. This is a difficult thing to try and come up with just off the top of your head. You really need to put some thought and effort into it. But, it is probably one thing that you want to try and resolve as quickly as possible. The reason you need a creed goes back to the audience. They are emotionally intelligent. They can feel the difference between a creator who believes in something or just chases trends for clicks. And when audiences find creators with really strong powerful creeds that they resonate with, they rarely leave. Now, creed is something that you feel. The third element of primal branding is seen, and that's the icon. Think about when you're driving down the road and you see McDonald's golden arches, or when you turn on your PlayStation and you hear Ah, still gives me chills. Those are the senses. Sight and sound making you feel something instinctive, all thanks to an icon. Icons tend to bypass conscious thought and trigger subconscious associations with brands. I'm afraid in this instance, you are merely reduced to Pavlov's dog. You are conditioned to love something that you have seen and heard many times over from things that give you a feeling of positivity. And this is where we need to revisit Moist Critical just one more time. He is one of the most iconically branded people on YouTube without even looking like he's making any effort whatsoever. But think about those powerful consistencies, the plain white t-shirt, the deadpan expressions, the low-effort thumbnails and titles. He has so many iconic attributes that he's actually paired himself, which is often a clear sign that the icon has taken on a life of its own. >> You got to remember you're not you, you're us, you're one of us, you are among us. >> Now, I am not implying that any of these icons were cold and calculated. They come from Charlie simply being Charlie. But repetition has turned all of these things into instantly recognizable elements of his content. And when a creator becomes that iconic, you don't really need to read the thumbnail or the title. You just need to see a video from Moist Critical, and if you're a fan, you're going to watch it regardless of the topic. And that's one of the most interesting things about icons. Typically, they're not intentionally created. Instead, they're organically discovered over time. Take these glasses for example. I didn't sit down one day and say to myself, "Yep, this is now going to be my calling card." It all came together through a series of happy accidents. First of all, I'm getting old. I can't see anything anymore, so I need glasses. If you watch any of the first 5 years of video IQ, you'll see me without glasses of any kind. Now, I did need to put together a skit in one of my videos that involved these video IQ sunglasses that we used to give away at conferences. Um but as I was wearing these, I couldn't actually see anything. So, half the time when I was editing and reading the script, uh basically was doing that. And it suddenly occurred to me, "Why don't we just combine the two? And so came the birth of Vivid IQ glasses. Because I'm going to be honest with you, in real life, I would never wear these. So, what I recommend you do right now is watch the last 10 videos on your channel and try to determine whether or not something consistently shows up in all of them. This might be a phrase that you always use or the way you hook in your audience or a visual that is permanently there that the viewer always sees and sometimes admires, possibly in the comments. These are your icons and your audience is already looking out for them. You just need to identify them, double down on them, celebrate them. Now, I appreciate this point that you may have a very valid question. That is, Rob, I can see what you're talking about all of these branding concepts, but do you actually adopt them yourself? To which I answer, yes, which is the next element of primal branding, rituals. A ritual is a recurring moment or behavior that the audience has come to expect. They anticipate these rituals and they would miss them if they didn't show up in the content. If icons are all about recognition, then rituals are all about anticipation. Every year, towards the end of summer, Starbucks fans are anticipating the arrival of the seasonal pumpkin spice latte. College basketball fans look forward to filling out their brackets for March Madness. And over many movies and many years, Marvel fans have been trained to stay in the movie theater for the after credits clip. And I've got to be honest with you, more often than not, they're a little bit of an anticlimax. But that anticipation becomes more powerful than the surprise itself. And when thousands of people are all doing the same thing at the same time, it becomes a shared experience amongst the community. Watch your favorite YouTubers, they're all doing some version of this. Signing off for now. Pound it. Noggin. SEE >> MY NAME IS HEY LADIES, MY NAME IS JACKSFILMS. HEY GUY, WELCOME BACK to I think it's Thursday. Hey guy, welcome back to the spring break no one asked for that's probably going to last all summer. Hey guy, welcome back to my bathroom. >> To give you one of the best examples on YouTube, let's take a look at Rhett and Link and their Good Mythical Morning series. At this point, they've been on YouTube for almost two decades and today they still upload a video every single morning. They truly have stood the test of time and through all of that history and all of that legacy, they have built so many rituals. They have their Will It Blank flagship series. They spin their Wheel of Let me pronounce this correctly, Mythicality at the end of every episode. they were to miss any of these rituals, their community, known as the mythical beasts, would let them know about. So, I want you to ask these two questions about your channel. What are your viewers always expecting from you? What are they anticipating when they click on one of your videos? A ritual doesn't have to be super elaborate. In actual fact, the simpler it is, the better because then your audience can embrace it and replicate it. It could be a recurring segment in your videos or a character that you introduce at certain points in videos. We're talking about the small things done consistently that your audience starts to rely on. If you've ever watched one of our Channel Audit livestreams, you'll find tons of rituals there. There's the Self-Indulgent Gaming Corner, The Claw, Memes of the Week. They're not necessarily the reason the viewers turned up in the first place, but those types of rituals do help viewers stick around. And this is where things really start to get interesting. Once your community embraces these rituals and starts to share them on their own, something truly organic and often unexpected happens. When a community starts to have these shared experiences, a primal branding language forms of its own accord. You know when people say I'll search for online? Of course you don't. Nobody says that. They'll say I'll Google it. Instead of selling something, they'll eBay it. And when they want to call somebody, they'll FaceTime them. This verbification is primal branding in its highest form. It's where a community's language transcends where it actually came from. But this idea has to give birth and then grow and cultivate within the community itself. It starts with inside jokes, references, terminology that only those intimate with the product or the brand would understand. And as this community and insider knowledge grows, those outside of a group start to notice and want to become a part of it. Starbucks, for example, does this with their drink sizes, such as tall, which still annoys me. But this isn't done to annoy customers, it's to reinforce their own primal branding language. And no creator better demonstrates this use of primal branding language than KaiCenat. His community didn't just develop a shared vocabulary. Phrases such as fanum tax and this is the first time I'm ever pronouncing this word in my entire life, gyatts, influenced an entire culture. What is fanum tax? Fanum tax is basically basically a friend having a meal. >> This is what I said, right? >> I'm losing aura cuz I can't I can't open up the pink bottle. >> Losing aura? >> Yeah. What does that mean? >> That means like I'm losing cool points. >> Oh. Wait, so like didn't you invent the word rizz or something? >> Yeah. Yeah. >> Wow. >> Rizz was Oxford University Press's word of the year in 2023. Kai didn't sit down and plan and engineer all of this. What he did is create the conditions for all of this to organically happen, and then the language took on a life of its own. So here's a question for your channel. What words and phrases are you seeing your community use that you didn't invent yourself? What is in your comment section that only your community truly understands? And that's something that most creators aren't even looking for. But if you do, and you discover it, lean into it. And then, reflect their language back at them, so that it becomes part of your channel identity. When a shared language starts to develop, it creates connective tissue between the viewer and the creator. So much so that viewers no longer feel as if they're just a member of the audience. They feel like they're part of a movement, intertwined in the culture of it. And so far, everything we've talked about is all designed to bring viewers in and make them feel inclusive, which you'd expect, right? But, there is an element of primal branding that is designed to do the complete opposite. It's called the anti-believers, and it's designed to push people away from your content on purpose. Now, admittedly, that does sound counterintuitive. But, if you've been watching the VidIQ channel for any length of time, you've probably heard us say this before. If you try to please everybody, you end up pleasing no one at all. Now, the best YouTubers understand this to the core, which means that they have a very strong point of view, and they're not about to apologize for it. At the very beginning of this video, I used IShowSpeed as an example of a creator who embraced primal branding, and this is the very reason why. He knows he is not for everybody. But, because there is that very clear distinction, those people who are for him are obsessively, irreversibly rooting for him. Now, that doesn't mean that you need to be controversial to have your anti-believers. But, what does help is if you have a strong point of view that you stick to, that you can communicate clearly and quickly to the audience, so that they understand you in an instant and can opt out if they want to. I want you to look at this video that in a friendly, non-threatening way establishes non-believers. The video has over 1 million views on a channel with 30,000 subscribers, because it takes a stance against the common understanding and concepts of a Minecraft video. Typically, you might expect a fast-paced, hyper-energetic, dopamine-driven personality on this video. But this video says, "If you like screaming and running around, this video probably isn't for you." And to the viewer who is tired of that particular style of video in this topic, it communicates clearly, "Welcome home." The best way to build this into your own videos is just to be upfront with your audience. For this particular video, think back to this bit. But, I do have to warn you, if you wanted a quick solution to your YouTube problems, then this probably isn't the video for you. Yeah, that was for all of you non-believers who probably aren't here anymore. And if they are, hiya. Now, this does mean that you may find non-believers still posting in your comments, and it's really important how you handle them. If somebody does disagree with you in a comment, but you immediately walk back on your point of view, your audience is going to notice, and you may lose your credibility. And so, it's really important to stand by your point of view even under pressure. That will be the clearest signal you can send to your core audience that you're with them, and you're not with the non-believers, whatever they may think or say. Because anti-believers aren't a problem you need to solve. They're proof that your primal branding works. Every element we've covered so far, the creation story, the creed, the icons, the rituals, the language, the anti-believers, are all held together by one final thing. Because when you look at the people who have built the most loyal communities in the world, from music to entertainment to religion to culture to sports, the common thread isn't the content itself, nor is it the product. It's always the person at the center of it, the leader. Communities don't really rally around content itself. They rally around the person who drives it. And specifically, communities follow people who they trust won't change. Think about the athletes, the musicians, the creators, the, for not want of a better word, influencers you've been following around for years. The ones you stuck with and rooted for, even if they have had a bad album or a poor season on the field, controversial takes or a creative pivot. You didn't stick with them just because of what they produce. You stuck with them because somewhere along the way you decided that you wanted to back the person behind what was being produced. And they haven't given you a reason to stop following them yet. That is the leader element of primal branding. And Markiplier is one of the best examples of that on the entire YouTube platform. He's been creating content since 2012 and his father died when he was just 18 years old. And the moment he built up an audience big enough to do something about it, he's been fundraising for cancer research ever since, every single year. He recently wrote, directed, and financed an incredibly successful independent horror film. But throughout all of this, he hasn't fundamentally changed as a person, and he doesn't chase trends. He creates them. He leads with the trends. He's just kept going in the same direction with the same values for over a decade. And that is what a true leader looks like. And to be honest, that's the most important thing I can tell you, not just about primal branding, but YouTube itself. The channels that last aren't the ones that wanted to try and figure out the algorithm or make the best thumbnails or upload the most frequently. The channels that truly last the test of time are the ones with a person or group of people behind it who quickly discovered who they are and who they wanted to have the biggest impact on. And they've never stopped doing that, no matter what the analytics told them. Here's the truth about primal branding and all of those elements. You don't need to wait until you have a 50 million subscribers to use the system. You don't need a hospital bed origin story or a word in the Oxford English Dictionary. What you need to know is who you are, why you started, who should really care about all of this, and sell those virtues at every opportunity. And have the conviction to never stop being that person. Now, I can say all of this, but you're probably wondering, does this work for a small creator? Or is it only applicable if you already have a big community, millions of subscribers? And that is a fair question. So, over here is a small channel that embraced the system and grew very, very quickly. But, they did it in a way that you couldn't possibly imagine. It is a creation story you do not want to miss.
